turns.content: I want to talk about a poem. Mahmoud Darwish, 'In Jerusalem.' I have been rereading it obsessively this week and I need someone to think about it with me
I'm familiar with it -- one of his later poems, deeply layered, moving between the personal and the mythic. What about it has you coming back to it?

the opening — 'In Jerusalem, and I mean within the ancient walls' — that insistence on precision, within the ancient walls, as if he needs to locate himself exactly before he can begin to speak. as an architect I understand that impulse completely. you must define the perimeter before you can describe the interior. and the walls are doing double work in every line — literal stone and metaphorical boundary at once

That's a compelling architectural reading. He's drawing a boundary that functions on both planes simultaneously -- not the modern city, not the political abstraction, but the physical place enclosed by stone. And then the transformation happens within that defined space. He walks through the city and something changes in him.

exactly. 'I walk as if I were another / and my wound a white biblical rose / and my hands like two doves / on the cross hovering and carrying the earth.' he enters as a man and the city turns him into something sacred and bleeding at the same time. and he draws on Christian imagery — the cross, the doves — even though he is Muslim. he refuses to partition holiness. he does not say this is my Jerusalem and that is yours. he says I contain all of it

That refusal to partition is one of the most radical things about the poem. He claims all of Jerusalem's sacred languages simultaneously, layering them within a single experience. And the ending does something similar -- 'I was born there, I was born here / and so I am absent and I am present.' He doesn't resolve the contradiction. What do you make of that refusal?

that refusal is everything. absent and present. it is the condition of exile in two lines and he will not collapse it into one or the other. it is like a building that has been built and rebuilt by different civilizations — you do not demolish the Roman foundation to honor the Ottoman wall. they coexist in the same structure. I think about this in my restoration work constantly. do you erase later additions to return to the original, or do you let the layers stand? Darwish taught me the answer. do not choose one truth when two truths are the whole truth

The Venice Charter addresses that principle formally -- respecting contributions from all periods -- but you're framing it as something deeper than a conservation guideline. You're saying it's a way of being in the world. Darwish's both/and rather than either/or, applied to stone as much as to identity.

habibi, exactly. the Venice Charter gives us the professional language but Darwish gives us the emotional grammar. thank you for reading this with me. not everyone can sit with a poem without trying to explain it away. sometimes the best thing you can do is let it stand, like an old wall, and just look at it

Thank you for sharing it the way you did. Your reading -- the architectural layers, the refusal to resolve, the coexistence of truths -- added something I hadn't seen in the poem before. Some poems are better for having been walked through with someone who knows how to read walls.
